title: Te Hauora o te Awa: The Health of Our River / Te Hauora o te Awa: Te Oranga o Tō Tātou Awa
subject: Health & Physical Education
culturalContext: Exploring the connection between the health of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream and the wellbeing of Ngāti Kahungunu through traditional practices of kaitiakitanga (guardianship), mahinga kai (food gathering), and wai ora (water health)
learningObjectives: Analyze how the health of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream directly impacts the physical, mental, and spiritual wellbeing of our local community
Apply traditional Ngāti Kahungunu practices of kaitiakitanga to design and implement a stream health monitoring activity
Create a personal wellbeing plan that incorporates connection to te taiao (the environment) through regular interaction with our local waterway
Collaborate with local kaumātua to document and preserve traditional knowledge about sustainable food gathering practices from the awa

activities.title: Whakawhanaungatanga ki te Awa: Connecting to Our River
activities.description: Students begin with a karakia at the banks of Mangakōtukutuku Stream, learning the pūrākau (traditional story) of how the stream got its name from the kōtukutuku (tree fuchsia) that grows along its banks. They make observational drawings and record initial thoughts about the stream's health

activities.title: Nga Mahi a Rongo: Traditional Food Gathering Practices
activities.description: Local kaumātua guide students in identifying traditional mahinga kai sites along the stream, explaining seasonal gathering practices for tuna (eels), kōura (crayfish), and watercress. Students learn how these practices maintained both stream health and community nutrition

activities.title: Te Tirohanga Hauora: Stream Health Assessment
activities.description: Using both mātauranga Māori indicators (presence of native species, water clarity) and scientific measures (pH testing, macroinvertebrate survey), students conduct a comprehensive health assessment of three different sites along the Mangakōtukutuku Stream

activities.title: Hīkoi Hauora: Wellbeing Walk and Reflection
activities.description: Students participate in a silent walk along the stream, stopping at designated points to reflect on how connection to this place affects their own hinengaro (mental), tinana (physical), wairua (spiritual), and whānau (social) wellbeing

activities.title: He Rautaki Kaitiaki: Guardianship Action Plan
activities.description: In small groups, students develop and present practical action plans for improving stream health, incorporating both traditional knowledge and modern science, with selected plans to be implemented in partnership with local iwi environmental groups

resources: Ngāti Kahungunu kaumātua and knowledge holders from local marae
Mangakōtukutuku Stream historical health data from Hawke's Bay Regional Council
'Te Mātauranga o Ngāti Kahungunu mo te Awa' traditional knowledge resource
Stream health testing kits from Whitebait Connection education program

tasks: Create a detailed stream health report comparing traditional Māori and scientific indicators
Develop a personal wellbeing plan that incorporates regular connection to the awa
Present a kaitiakitanga action proposal to local kaumātua and community representatives

nzcAlignment: Health and Physical Education: A3 - Safety management; B3 - Interpersonal skills; C3 - Community resources; D3 - Societal attitudes and values
Social Sciences: Understand how people manage resources; Understand how people remember and record the past
Science: Living World: Ecology; Planet Earth and Beyond: Earth systems
